title: Chapter 29: First Blood
content: The sound came first. A clicking. Metal on concrete, rhythmic and precise, echoing through the access tunnel like a heartbeat with no body. Wren froze mid-step, her hand still resting on the damp corridor wall.
The clicking grew louder.

Petra heard it too. Twenty meters ahead in the tunnel junction, the young woman turned her head toward the sound. Her lips parted. No words came out — just a thin exhale of recognition, of animal fear.
Then Wren saw it.

Matte-black. Low-slung. Four legs that moved with the hideous fluidity of something that had studied locomotion but never felt it. A mechanical hound, roughly the size of a German shepherd, its head a smooth dome studded with sensor arrays that glowed faint red in the darkness. No eyes. Just lenses. Just calculation.
A scout unit. The first of the pack.
Petra ran.
The hound was faster.

Wren's legs locked. She wanted to scream, to move, to do something — but her body understood before her mind did that speed was not her weapon here. The hound closed the distance in three seconds. Not a lunge. A pursuit. Efficient. Designed.

Petra made it forty meters before the hound's jaw-clamp closed around her ankle. She went down hard, palms scraping concrete, her scream tearing through the tunnel like something ripped from fabric. The sound was raw. Human. Final.
"Help!" Petra shrieked. "Somebody—"

The hound didn't kill her. That was the worst part. It pinned her with mechanical precision, a foreleg across her back, and emitted a single pulse — a subsonic frequency that Wren felt in her molars, in the bones of her wrists. A recall signal.

Within minutes, the containment team arrived. White van. No markings. Two people in tactical gear, faces behind visors. They moved like surgeons. One sedated Petra — a pen injector to the neck, quick and clinical. The other loaded her onto a stretcher. She was unconscious before the van door closed.
The whole extraction took less than four minutes.

Wren watched from behind a junction box, her hand over her mouth, her fingernails biting into her own cheek. Around her, she could feel the others — she couldn't see them, but she felt their held breath, their frozen terror. Silas somewhere to her left. A woman named Darby behind. A boy of sixteen called Mouse — close enough that Wren could hear his ragged, shallow breathing.
No one moved.

The van pulled away. The hound remained for another thirty seconds, scanning. Its red sensors swept the tunnel in a slow arc. Wren pressed herself against the concrete and willed herself to disappear — not just invisibly, but entirely. To stop existing. To become the wall.
The hound turned. Clicked away into the darkness from which it came.
Silence. Then the tunnel exhaled.
"She's gone," someone whispered. Darby, maybe. The voice cracked like thin ice.

Wren's hands were shaking. Not a tremor — a vibration. Something inside her was building, pressure behind her sternum, electricity climbing her spine. She'd felt glitch-power surge before, but this was different. This wasn't a ripple. This was a wave.
The lights went out.

Not just in the tunnel. Every fluorescent tube, every emergency bulb, every LED strip across the visible corridor — all of them died at once. The pop and fizz of burning filaments echoed like firecrackers. Glass cracked. Something in the wall panel behind her sparked and caught briefly before smothering itself in the damp.
Total darkness.
Wren gasped. Her hands were still shaking. The vibration hadn't stopped — it had just found no outlet, no target. It roamed through her like a caged thing, looking for something to break.
"Did you do that?" Silas's voice, hoarse, close.
"I don't know." Her own voice sounded far away. "I don't—"
Another light popped. Then another. Down the corridor, a cascade of small explosions, each one further away, like dominoes falling in reverse. The sound was hideous — sharp, percussive, final.
Then nothing. Just darkness and the smell of ozone and burning plastic.
Mouse was crying. Quietly, the way invisible people learn to cry — no sound, just the shudder of a body trying to disappear from itself.
Wren sank to the floor. The concrete was cold. The cold was real. She held onto it.

Petra was gone. Not dead — worse. Witnessed. Contained. Sedated and loaded into a white van like damaged inventory, like a product being recalled. The machine had found her, pinned her, and called for pickup, and the whole thing had taken less time than it took to microwave a meal.
This was what NeuroScale had built. Not an execution. An extraction. They weren't killing invisibles.
They were collecting them.

Wren's hands were still buzzing. She pressed them flat against the concrete and felt the resonance drain slowly, reluctantly, back into the ground. The lights stayed dead. She'd killed them. Every one.
First uncontrolled burst. Stage 4, creeping in like dawn.
Ash found her ten minutes later. He didn't speak — just sat beside her in the darkness. His shoulder against hers. Warm. Solid. Alive.
She leaned into him. Not for comfort. For proof.
"The hound will come back," Ash said quietly. "That was a scout. It mapped the tunnels. It'll relay the data."
"I know."
"Petra—"
"I know."

He was quiet for a long time. His hand found hers in the dark. Their fingers interlaced. Neither of them squeezed. They just held. Two invisible people in a dead corridor, holding on to the only thing they could still feel.
"It's real now," Wren said.
"Yeah." His breath was warm against her temple. "It's real."

Somewhere deeper in the Underlayer, someone had started crying out Petra's name. Calling for her in the dark, the way you call for someone who's already gone. The sound bounced off the concrete and came back changed — thinner, emptier, a name with no body attached.
Just like them. Names with no bodies. Soon to be bodies with no names.
Wren closed her hand tighter around Ash's. The buzzing in her palms had faded, but she could still feel it — coiled, waiting. A wire with too much current. A switch with no off position.
The hound had found Petra in four minutes.
In two weeks, there would be more than one.
